title: Why Early Maps Were So Wrong and Still So Useful

lesson.learn:
Early maps were often inaccurate because mapmakers had limited data, few standardized measurements, and no satellite view of the planet. Some regions were guessed, others were copied from older sources, and many coastlines were drawn from travelers' stories rather than direct surveying.

That did not make the maps pointless. In their own time, they were useful tools for trade, travel, religion, and empire. A wrong map can still be better than no map at all if it helps people make decisions.
lesson.simpler: Early maps were wrong because people had incomplete information. Even so, they were still useful for travel and trade.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Old maps were limited by missing data and bad projections, not by a lack of intelligence.
lesson.deeperDive:
Some maps failed because the world was only partly known. Others failed because they tried to flatten a sphere onto a rectangle. The Mercator projection is a classic example: it preserves direction well for navigation but greatly distorts size near the poles. Medieval T-O maps were not meant to function like modern atlases at all; they often organized the world around symbolic and religious ideas.

In that sense, map history is really a history of changing purpose. As surveying improved, maps became more precise. As exploration expanded, the map had to keep changing with it. Every generation had to redraw the world from the evidence it had.
lesson.funFact: A map can be wrong and still be the best available tool.

people: Surveyors - people who measure land and coasts
Navigators - users of maps at sea
Historians - researchers who compare old maps and records
places: Medieval Europe - home to many symbolic world maps
Port cities - centers of navigation and charting
Surveying sites - where measurements were gathered
Royal collections - places that preserved valuable maps
years: 13th to 15th centuries - medieval map traditions dominate
Present day - map history is a major field of study
concepts: Projection - a method of flattening a globe
Scale - the relationship between map and real distance
Symbolic map - a map designed to convey meaning, not accuracy
Cartography - the art and science of mapmaking
